answer:
The argument has premises ~A · ~B and (A ∨ C) ∨ B, with conclusion C.  
If A, B, and C are all false, then ~A · ~B is true, (A ∨ C) ∨ B is false ∨ false = false, so the premises are not both true, making this not a counterexample.  
The correct counterexample is when A and B are false and C is true, because then ~A · ~B is true, (A ∨ C) ∨ B is (false ∨ true) ∨ false = true, but conclusion C is true, so actually the argument is valid.  

\boxed{H}
